sentences.text: Yes, after the war, yeah.
Yes, after me, to the country.
Yes, to Balaton.
Because she was a Jewish--
Of course, she was Jewish.
Because when she born, her mother was a Jewish, and her father was a Jewish.
But she was in Budapest, and-- because his-- her father live in Budapest, not Ujpest.
After they divorced, she-- he went.
No, she-- he died before.
He died in the-- in the-- in the Budapest ghetto.
Yeah.
Look.
I-- I don't mind, because if she wanted to live the same way like I do, but she don't.
She was a different type of person.
She was too ladylike.
That's what she think.
And-- and she don't like the [LAUGHS] moo cows and the [LAUGHS] things.
And I went to get her in the railway station.
And I went with the-- with the [NON-ENGLISH]-- with the-- with the moo cow was in the front, you know?
And I went in the front with the-- stick, and I-- [LAUGHS] --it was-- Pushing.
--no shoe, you know?
And I went to see her.
And I looking.
And she looking.
She don't think I am me.
And I don't think she's her, you know?
I said, what is that woman doing here?
[LAUGHS]
And then I go to her.
I said, what's your name?
My God, you are a [NON-ENGLISH]!
[LAUGHS]
And-- and that's how we meet.
But did she travel on her own papers?
No, no, no, no.
Yes, yes.
She have to.
Nobody can-- a Jewish person was not allowed to go in the train.
And they have to wear a star.
So, no, she--
No.
She don't.
Of course not, she was 1/2 Jewish, so she don't look so Jewish.
And she looks like-- exactly like her mother.
[LAUGHS] So--
Yes.
I was the-- the housekeeper-- next to the housekeeper.
[LAUGHS] I was a second housekeeper-- and cooking, and cleaning.
10 priests-- young priests.
Of-- of course, a church.
He build a church.
He built that church.
That Dr.
Roberts, he-- he build that church.
That was a-- a railway workers' church, a railway-- a railway workers' town.
All the people who working in a railway, they live in that town.
And he build that church.
He's-- he-- he-- he get the money.
He-- he went to America.
He speak English.
And he a very, very clever man.
Oh, yes.
And-- and help him to the-- to the church.
And one day was a wedding.
Of course, I have to prepare the wedding.
They bring the bride in, and you know, with the candles and the-- and it was my school friend.
I was in school together.
I think I dropped dead.
But she was so excited, she don't recognize me.
I give her the candles like that, you know, to hold it.
But it was such a experience.
I think I dropped dead.
[LAUGHS] But--
No, she don't recognize me.
Because--
Yeah, after, after.
Oh!
And we-- in the nighttime, we make the book.
And we give people a forged christening letters.
Who-- any-- any-- anybody who come and ask, we give it to-- of the-- the priest give it to him.
Only Jewish people.
Yes, yes.
No.
No.
Yes.
Yes, he always, all his life he-- he was sympathetic, because-- maybe that's why he was a friend of my father.
But he said, it's actually, we all coming from there, you know.
He was not a-- oh-- blinded Catholic.
I-- I mean, you know, he-- he knows Jesus Christ was Jew.
So?
Of course!
I-- I clean for them, you know.
And I cook for them.
And we was very, very friendly.
And we was-- have a-- a Jewish priest who was-- or Jewish boy, who become a priest.
And his family live in Budapest.
And he went to visit them.
And he never come back.
No.
No, no, no, no, no, no-- nobody, nobody-- no-- nobody ever expected, because I don't looks like, and I don't-- don't act like, you know.
Because I always was with-- with my friends, and-- and how I say?
The-- my-- mine grandfather wasn't a Jew, so I never was a typical Jewish girl.
[LAUGHS]
After when-- when the Russians are come in.
The February, 1945.
And I think in March time-- yes-- I-- I wanted to go home, because I wanted to fix our house.
I was hoping my mother maybe come back.
That time I don't know my mother not alive, you know?
And I was hoping my brother will come home.
He was only 22.
I don't know he'd-- what's happening to him.
And I don't want they should have to go, like I have to go to be a housekeeper, and do that, and do that.
I want-- I want to wait for them with the home the-- and a family, you know?
And I was hoping they coming home, but-- [SIGHS] it's not.
So-- but I don't know that time.
And I wanted to build up a-- a home for them.
That was all my effort-- to put the things together.
And how I said, we-- we take most of the furnishing to my father's place.
And I wanted to have a home.
So I find my cousin, one of my cousin coming home, and who is the-- or who was the richest in the-- in my town.
And she come home with her husband and two little girl.
And she find her fortune.
They have a-- a furnishing factory.
And she find the-- the factory, and was a-- a [NON-ENGLISH] Furnishing, which was a big effort at that time, and I think how many kilo of gold and things.
She--
She find it.
It was-- was under the ground.
And she knows where it was.
In the factory-- in their factory.
No, no, because of the Germans are working in the factory.
And they used to have the beautifulest home in the world-- so-- in the-- in the city, in my town, and the big common-- the-- the big-- [NON-ENGLISH] over there.
--commander, he live in their home.
So that she find everything-- the Persian carpets, the piano, and everything, because they live there.
And they put the-- the gold in their factory, because they knows they working there.
They will don't--
Yes, and they-- they don't think the stupid Jew will put it over there-- the-- the jewelry, you know?
And he did.
And she find it.
Yeah.
Oh, yes.
Oh, yes.
Yes.
I feel thanks for them, you know, because if they not come, we are not surviving, you know?
But--
Look.
It's-- I always said, if you take-- keep the distance from them, they was all right.
But if you started to friendly and not friendly, and you know-- it-- it-- it depends how you act.
That-- that was my opinion.
And I was a-- a younger, pretty girl.
And they don't want me.
They-- [LAUGHS] because I don't give them a chance to look at me that way.
They always see me working, and you know, do that, and do that, and cleaning.
So it was no hanky panky in my eyes.
So they-- they leave me alone.
They-- they go to the other people who-- who want a hanky panky.
They was-- they was nice to me.
I can say that.
So you continued working for the priest?
Yes, till-- till March, you know?
Then I go to my cousin place.
And I--
No, because somebody lived-- yeah, of course.
She live in Ujpest. too.
Yeah.
I-- I no.
Because people lived there.
Had of course-- how the-- when we move out, they moved in.
Yeah.
Not in the first few weeks.
No, I can't.
I stayed with my cousin.
She go back to her place.
It's a beautiful home.
And-- and I was her housekeeper.
And I could-- the-- the children.
She has two children that time.
And I cooking, and I cleaning.
And they-- they have to give one room to the Russian soldier.
So I clean after them, and have-- them-- they-- they bring me some food, [LAUGHS] make business with them.
I mean to-- to the children--
--have a food, because there was no food-- no food after the war.
We go to the [NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah.
No, no.
The Red Cross-- the Red Cross.
But that was only a few-- few days, a few weeks, you know?
And after, when we get-- we go to the black market, and there she has the gold.
So we get the food.
And we can go back to normal.
And I-- I cooking and cleaning.
And then I find it in the basement-- I find a machine-- of-- with the--
--sewing machine.
And I said to her, do you know what?
I started to make some clothes to the children, to her children.
And then I started to make her a-- a frock.
And I said to her, you know what?
I started to be sewing, and I will keep a housekeeper for you.
And I will make a-- a living.
Because-- And you-- you still lived with her, though?
Oh, yes.
Yes.
And I working with the-- to the Russian woman, you know, the baryshnyas.
They have no taste and no-- don't know what to wear and how to wear it.
And they bring me some nightgowns to make an evening frock out it.
And I do it for them.
And they bring me sugar, and ducks, and chickens, and things.
And I was a rich girls, you know, because I have food.
And it was everything-- everything for the food at that time to surviving.
And I even get some jams.
And I-- I make a parcel, and I go to the priest.
I give it to them and bring it to them.
And my cousin said, what you doing?
I said, they was good to me.
I have to.
I-- the first thing, I take some food for them, what they-- what I earned with the Russians.
How long did you continue doing that for?
I do it that about till May.
And then I meet my mother girlfriend who was a-- a-- our lawyer's sister.
And she live behind our house.
And she has a empty flat.
She said to me, you know, come here.
Live with me in this-- not-- not together.
I have a separate flat, you know?
She said, make it nice, and you are my girlfriend daughter.
You are not alone, and I am not alone.
She lost her family.
Only she was alone.
She has a cousin with her, a-- a man.
And then how I earned the money-- I-- not the money, I mean the food-- so I meet a old friend who was a painter.
I said, look.
I give you two kilo sugar.
Paint my flat.
So he does.
And then I meet a man, and-- another man who was a furniture making.
I said, look.
I have my furnishing, just need to be re-polished and things.
I give you a two chicken and-- [LAUGHS] and a two kilo flour.
And he, said, fix it.
So he was happy.
So I fixed it up nicely, the flat too.
But-- well, I get the-- what-- I have to fight for it, you know.
He said, the Russians are take it.
I said, where?
So I asked my cousin husband.
I said come with me, because my dining room they said they take it over there.
So I take a carriage.
And I was the horse.
I pulled the carriage.
And I went house by house to looking my furnishing.
And I went in, and I find the cupboard-- a beautiful dining room.
In somebody else's place?
In somebody else's place, yes.
And it was one door was missing.
You know, it was-- it was a door who open up and was a office table.
You know, that time it was-- that was a fashion.
And I don't find the-- the door.
I said to my cousin husband, I said, it must be here.
And I looking everywhere.
And I said-- and I heard-- in the shower, I heard a noise.
And I went in.
And it was a naked Russian showering, and step on my door, you know?
And I went in.
I pull him [LAUGHS] up here.
I take off the [LAUGHTER] door.
And I lock-- close the door.
And I have my furnishing.
I have my doors.
And he was so surprised.
He [LAUGHS] was surprised, not me.
And I runaway and take my carriage like I was the horse.
And-- and I had my furnishing.
And-- and I was hoping my family coming home.
And--
Not yet, no.
Only-- only in-- in August, the people coming home, you know, more and more people.
And then they bring the news to the--
Yes, but I tell you honestly.
They was not so straight.
And I don't like not straight people.
They said to me, yes, we give you that, and that.
But-- and I don't like the but.
To be nice to me.
You know, the boys, the-- the mens over there, and I was young-- and to be nice, to be-- come out with me.
And I said, no, I don't want it.
I only want which is mine.
And I will get it.
No, I back my name.
Of course, I back my name.
And then one day my-- that girlfriend of mine who's mother bring some food to my mother, they-- their house was bombed.
And she has no home.
So I tell-- told her, I said, come and live with me in the meantime, you know, when you can get your own feet.
Because his parents live in the country.
They went to the country, because that-- they have no home.
And after the Russians, the-- you know, that part of the people was pushed away, you know, that the-- who were--
Yeah.
And then not only the rich people, but that-- that other-- the-- the-- who was serving the-- the German politics.
And her father was there, even if they don't think the same way, but they work in the parliament, you know?
And-- and they-- they push him away.
So she need my help.
So I help her.
She come with me, yes.
No.
You know, the people are funny.
They-- they live their own life.
And even how I always said, every non-Jews have a Jew friend, but they don't care so much-- not everybody.
They-- they care, but they don't go out of their way.
How shall I say?
You know what I mean?
Like after the war, I was thinking maybe they can tell me to go to the country with them, but they didn't.
In a way-- in a way, but then how my mother teach me and my [NON-ENGLISH] husband understand me, you can't change the people-- what they are.
You have to take them how they are.
You can't change them.
So that's-- that's how I-- I take it.
And-- and one day, we went for a walk with my girlfriend that-- who live with me.
And we meet my darling husband in the street.
And he straight away come to me, and how I am, and how is my brother.
And when somebody ask me my brother and my mother, I was already out of my way, you know?
I was so happy to talk about them.
And he said that because he was a scout, and my brother was a scout.
And-- and he take us home.
And he said, can I come to see you?
I said, why not?
You know?
And then he started to come.
And I cook him a nice chicken soup.
And you know, and he was alone.
He lost his family.
And my girlfriend said, that's the boy for you.
I said, Cat, how can you say that?
That's a-- we are not thinking of that way yet, you know, it's-- I was so, so upset-- the whole things.
I was not over it yet, you know?
And then the coming Rosh Hashanah-- and I light the candles from front of my family's picture.
And my husband come in, and I was crying.
And I said to him, I said, why I survive for what?
I am alone here.
And he said, to make me happy.
And that's how we get together, really.
That's the story of my life.
Yes.
We married in-- in September.
And he said, he-- he want to marry me.
And that he said, but not yet.
I said, no.
I not-- don't need a boyfriend.
Sorry.
We get married.
He said, when?
I said, I take you to the priest, and I will tell you.
And I take him over there to introduced him.
To the priest?
To the priest, of course.
Had he--
--was my father.
Yes?
And he said, and when you want to get married?
I said, in four weeks time.
My husband look at me, [LAUGHS] and he said, and how?
I said, I will fix it.
And I ask, and I fix it.
In four weeks time we was married.
Yes, in-- in his place, in the priest's place.
No.
Yeah.
And then--
The priest.
Yeah!
Yeah.
So I married three times.
So that's why my marriage working.
First time married in the town hall.
Then I married in his place.
Yeah.
And then before we come out from Hungary, we go to the Rabbi, and we have a ceremony.
So I have three weddings.
And that's why my marriage was for 45 years so nice and good.
And I never tell that anybody.
I only telling you.
Even my children don't know we-- I have three marriage.
[LAUGHS] I never tell them.
Yeah.
[LAUGHTER] Maybe we leave it out, then.
Yes, yes.
I have my little girl born there around-- she was one year old.
We-- because they ask me.
They wanted to give me some positions to the-- [NON-ENGLISH].
Yeah.
And-- and I don't want it.
No.
It was not my way life.
And my husband, my dear husband said, look, mommy, we have to go away, because you tell what you want to say.
And that's no good for you.
They put you in jail.
So-- and then--
He has a-- a leather shop.
Yes.
And he earned a very good money.
We was very--
Yes, his own shop with his cousin.
They was partners.
No, no, no, he was in the perfume business.
He selling before the war.
But he-- he's-- he learned, because every Jewish boy have to have something--
--a trade, so he learned a gloves making.
He was a very good gloves maker.
And then after the war, he-- he was working-- given the perfume.
He loved those things that, you know, he was very particular.
And after the war, he opened the shop, leather shop, with his cousin.
And he make a very good money.
And we live nicely and comfortable.
And then--
Yeah, yeah.
We walk.
We walk for two [NON-ENGLISH].
We go to--
--two borders to Czechoslovakia, and then Czechoslovakia to Vienna.
With my one-year-old baby.
We pay the men who carry him-- carry her, but when she started to cry, they give it to me.
So I have to carry her.
But--
Oh, yeah.
It was a nightmare.
And-- and even-- when we went to Czechoslovakia, then that was a shock of my life.
The-- the men who we paid a good dollars in Hungary, he disappeared and let us down.
And then we have to find ourselves.
And we don't speak-- we only speak Hungarian.
And we find somebody who said, for money, we take for my little girl, a dolly.
And we put some dollars and gold in the-- in the-- in the dolly, inside.
So every time when somebody asking, we opened the dolly and we give some money.
And [LAUGHS] we-- we go further and further.
And they put us in one room.
And they put a cupboard in front of it.
And they said in 24 hours you can be here, and then you have to go.
So next night was Friday night.
And I said to myself, I go to the synagogue.
You don't, daddy.
I go.
So I went to the synagogue, and I looking around-- Where was this?
In which town?
In-- in Kosice.
And I said, I was looking around in the synagogue, and I go to one of the men.
And I said, look.
We are coming from Hungary.
I have a little girl and a husband here.
We want to go through further.
Could you help us?
He said, you know, you let somebody buy you a ticket to the--
--railway.
And you go to [NON-ENGLISH] And from [NON-ENGLISH], you can go further, to Vienna.
